name: The Greek Alphabet
description: All 24 Greek letters in order, uppercase and lowercase. See the glyphs, type the name. Extras give the sound, the Latin-lookalike traps (nu is not v, rho is not p), and where each letter earns its keep in math, stats, and ML papers.

fields.extra: Sounds like 'a'. First letter; 'alphabet' is just alpha + beta. In papers: significance level, learning rates, angles.
Sounds like 'b'; uppercase looks like Latin B. In papers: regression coefficients, the beta distribution, Adam's momentum parameters.
Sounds like 'g'; uppercase is the right-angle hook. In papers: the discount factor in RL, the gamma function, gamma radiation.
Sounds like 'd'; uppercase is the triangle. In papers: uppercase for change or difference, lowercase for small quantities and the Dirac/Kronecker delta.
Sounds like a short 'e'. In papers: an arbitrarily small positive quantity, epsilon-greedy exploration, machine epsilon.
Sounds like 'z'. The Riemann zeta function. With xi, one of the two squiggles nobody can handwrite; zeta is the SIMPLER squiggle (one loop).
Sounds like a long 'ay'. Double trap: uppercase looks like Latin H, lowercase looks like n with a tail. In papers: the learning rate, efficiency.
Sounds like 'th'; the circle with a bar. In papers: THE symbol for model parameters, plus angles everywhere; Big-Theta bounds in CS.
Sounds like 'i'; the smallest letter, hence 'not one iota'. Rare in math precisely because it vanishes next to i.
Sounds like 'k'. In papers: Cohen's kappa for inter-rater agreement, condition numbers, curvature.
Sounds like 'l'. In papers: eigenvalues, regularization strength, Poisson rates; lambda calculus gave anonymous functions their name.
Sounds like 'm'; uppercase looks like Latin M. In papers: the mean; also the micro- prefix (a micrometer is a μm).
The classic trap: lowercase looks exactly like Latin v, but nu is the Greek N. In papers: frequency in physics, degrees of freedom in stats.
Sounds like 'ksee'. The notorious triple squiggle (zeta only has one loop); uppercase is three horizontal bars. In papers: a favorite for random variables and noise terms.
Literally 'small o' (o-micron), vs omega, 'big o'. Indistinguishable from Latin o, so math avoids it; the world met it as a COVID variant.
Sounds like 'p'. Lowercase: 3.14159... and the policy in RL, π(a|s). Uppercase: the product operator, summation's multiplicative sibling.
Trap: looks like Latin p, but rho is the Greek R. In papers: correlation coefficients (Spearman's rho), density.
Sounds like 's'. Uppercase: summation. Lowercase: standard deviation (σ² is variance) and the sigmoid's namesake. A third form, ς, appears only at the end of Greek words.
Sounds like 't'. In papers: Kendall's tau, time constants, and often the temperature in a softmax.
Sounds like 'u'. Rare in math, but a prolific parent: Latin U, V, W, and Y all descend from it.
Sounds like 'f'. In papers: feature maps φ(x), the golden ratio, angles; uppercase is the standard normal CDF. Also written ϕ; same letter.
Sounds like 'kh'. Looks like Latin x: that resemblance gave us the chi-squared (χ²) test's symbol and 'Xmas' (Χ for Christ).
Sounds like 'ps'; the trident. In papers: the wavefunction in quantum mechanics; the emblem of psychology (psi for psyche).
Literally 'big o'; the last letter ('alpha and omega'). Uppercase: ohms, and the whole sample space in probability. Lowercase: one outcome ω in that space, angular frequency.
